fragments.text: Tina, 18, alone in the back of an Uber, studies a translucent pink lychee gummy and says the choice out loud: "Should I peel it? Or eat it?"
As Tina bites, the tops and sides of the car peel away in candy-colored panels while a rainbow parachute opens behind her, catches the wind, and lifts her out.
Director red note: a giant hand reaches down and peels open the car like a fruit, on an ordinary neighborhood street.
KABOOSH — an explosion of anime fireworks and orange lightning engulfs Raul; tiny mango faces hover over his eyes.
Mango arms and legs dock onto Raul like Voltron modules until he stands as a blocky orange-and-yellow robot against a lightning-crossed field.
Director red note: a giant version of Raul leans down into frame and eats him.
The camera dives into Meg's awed open mouth — wide-angle extreme close-up of mouth, face, one eye — and emerges from darkness into an overhead of the school quad.
Liz and John lock eyes mid-stride on campus; a circular dolly orbits them as they drift together and the world BOOSHes into a mango grove full of dancing fruit characters.
BOOSH — the couple is suddenly underwater in black scuba gear, still holding their candy bags, surrounded by bright mango-orange fish and coral.
In flat-shaded old-school animation, Liz and John fly hand-in-hand diagonally over the campus, bags still in hand.
Super wide above the clouds: giant peeled-mango islands float everywhere; Tina's parachute, Raul's mango robot, and a giant candy bag all fly in to join them.
Both layers are edible: a fruit-shaped gummy whose outer skin peels off a softer center — no foil, no paper anywhere. The peel is the first bite.
A molded seam ridge where the skin parts — the device of a thumbnail catching the seam works at any scale.
The peel stretches, never tears: taut, then thinning, a translucent glow at the stress point, then release with a wobble.
Two textures as the product's visual signature: soft matte skin outside, denser translucent core inside.
At large scale, stretch-and-glow membrane language drifts fleshy and body-horror; the antidote is sugar-dusted, jewel-translucent, toy-glossy candy light.
World-scale extrapolation: buildings stretch before they peel, peels fall up instead of down, and everything peeled is edible at any scale.
Ben: every time a candy is eaten, "a creative situation" occurs — the eating itself is the trigger, and the situation can be anything.
The only fixed anchors are four ordinary teenage places: the back of a car, bleachers at a football game, campus steps, and a candy-islands finale.
A jungle sprouts in the middle of the city; dull houses flush bright tropical Cuban colors and grow murals as the transformation spreads outward.
The car grows larger in scale than the neighborhood while the houses become simplified toy models — the peel plays out giant-against-miniature.
A crew of tiny lychee gummies pulls the car roof off, rides the peel as it becomes a pink parasail, and tows Tina up into the sky.
The gummies never tear metal: they create seams and pull them open after the car's surface first turns soft and peel-like, then unfurls like an orange peel, flower petals, a lotus.
Dark-doppelganger alternate: three Tinas — one bites, one peels, and the middle one rises in the parasail while left and right depart on different adventures.
Tina sees the sky itself peel back, corner first, like a lifted film — reality can peel away.
Raul is briefly replaced by a giant mango gummy that closes around him, then he bursts out in lightning, shedding it like a cocoon.
A firework burst of little grinning mango guys coalesces into Raul's arms and legs — he is literally made of his own minions.
A mango pit crew stretches, fits, and locks opaque mango-flesh armor components into place, piece by rehearsed piece.
Eat the center, wear the peel: Raul consumes the core and the skin becomes his wearable armor — the inside swallowed, the outside worn.
Reflection-first transformation: the change shows only in mirrors, windows, and chrome before it happens to the world itself.
Bite-as-sports-replay: every replayed bite releases one mango-flesh component and warps the matching football formation on the field below.
Kawaii lychee pair: a whole fruit with bumpy scaled crimson rind and a smiling face, beside a peeled half — pearly translucent white flesh cupped in its rind with a glossy dark seed at center.
Mango moodboard collage: real mangoes, hedgehog-cut cube grids, fuzzy plush mango characters with stitched smiles, mango bubble tea, and a toy poodle peeking out of a hollowed mango.
Two fluffy onigiri characters at a hilltop watch a third sled down the long green slope seated on its own nori sheet, beaming the whole way down.
A photo-real mango becomes a character with nothing but doodled black stick arms and legs, blush dots, and a happy open mouth, caught mid-dance.
Round green chicks hatch from dark grapes, wearing the half-peeled purple skins as hoods and pants; a bunch of intact glossy grapes sits nearby, faceless.
A fuzzy crayon-textured banana with a grumpy little face wears a round "Fresh 100%" sticker on its peel.
A sticker sheet of ten mangoes — whole, halved, hedgehog-cut, sliced — every form with sleepy blushing faces; one wears a Hello Kitty hood.
Dozens of soft white blob spirits with tiny tails and wide pink mouths tumble out of a mossy forest ledge in one happy pile.
Photoreal composite: a lovebird whose body is a ripe red-to-yellow mango — stem, leaf, and gray talons all continuous with the fruit, one eye where the blush deepens.
A glossy mango-yellow humanoid mecha model kit: gray articulated joints, twin shoulder cannons, caution decals, a black rifle, and a tiny pilot visible under the head canopy.
A chunky super-deformed yellow mecha with a green pineapple-crown of spikes, one green cyclops eye, and quad missile pods in its stubby arms — menacing and adorable at once.
Queneau's Cent mille milliards de poemes: ten sonnets cut into fourteen strips per page so any line combines with any — 10^14 valid poems inside one bound book.
Gysin discovered cut-ups while slicing through newspapers layered on a cutting mat — the accidental cross-column juxtapositions read better than the columns themselves.
Bowie's cut-up, "a kind of Western Tarot": paragraphs on deliberately different subjects sliced into four-word strips, shuffled, and reconnected into lyrics.
Exquisite corpse: players draw in turn on folded paper, each seeing only the connecting lines at the fold — first output: "The exquisite corpse shall drink the new wine."
Dali's paranoiac-critical double image: the face hiding in the rock, rendered photorealistically so both readings stay simultaneously true.
Decalcomania: press wet paint between two surfaces, peel them apart, and find the image waiting in the blot.
The Five Obstructions #2: Leth eats a gourmet meal in Mumbai's red-light district behind a translucent screen, the street crowd visible through the scrim.
Gondry's "Fell in Love with a Girl": the White Stripes built entirely of LEGO bricks, animated frame by frame, the blocky seams doing the charming.
Gondry's "Around the World": each band member's motion is a looping choreographed cycle, the whole video an Escher machine of synchronized repeats.
Cadbury "Gorilla": a gorilla sits at a drum kit through a long anticipatory wait, then nails the Phil Collins fill — no chocolate on screen until the endframe.
Sony Bravia "Balls": 250,000 bouncy balls released down a San Francisco hill, six cameras, all in-camera — dented cars and streets frothing with color.
Skittles "Touch": a man's touch turns everything to Skittles — a beloved party trick, but he can never hold his newborn.
Skittles rains from the ceiling indoors and everyone on screen treats the candy weather as completely unremarkable.
Skittles "uncomfortably soft": a balloon-animal dog scrapes itself along the carpet — softness pushed past pleasant into a new emotional register.
Skittles shoots real puppets and digitally removes the puppeteers — the slightly-wrong physical texture is itself the unsettling tone.
Old Spice in one apparent take: the voice never changes while bathroom becomes boat becomes horse and tickets become diamonds — "I'm on a horse" scavenged from a dead script.
Guinness "Surfer": the wave finally breaks as white horses out of a Walter Crane painting stampeding in the foam — good things come to those who wait.
Haribo: executives in a boardroom argue about gummies in dubbed children's voices — exactly one channel swapped, everything else documentary-real.
Snickers: mid-scrimmage, the hungry teammate is literally Betty White getting tackled into the mud — a different person until he eats.
Sour Patch Kids: sour coating then sweet interior becomes plot — the Kid commits mischief, then makes amends; the candy's taste sequence as a two-act arc.
Fruit Gushers: kids bite the gusher and their heads inflate into giant pieces of fruit.
Twix left/right: one identical candy bar split into two rival halves, mirrored factories feuding over which side is which.
M&M's spokescandies: anthropomorphic candies living in polite terror of being eaten by the very people they charm.
The gummy-category default shot: ASMR macro of an unwrap-and-peel with breathy sound design — tactile intimacy at extreme close-up.
